heroHeadline: Islamabad Talks Collapse After 21 Hours; Ceasefire Deadline at Midnight; Af-Pak Non-Escalation Holds Day 12
heroSubtitle: US negotiators left Islamabad on April 21 after 21 hours of failed Iran talks, with the US-Iran ceasefire set to expire at midnight April 22. Trump threatened Iran with consequences 'like they've never seen before'; Iran's parliament speaker vowed 'new cards on the battlefield.' Taliban official Zakir Jalaly publicly urged Iran to stop regional attacks, underlining Afghanistan's vulnerability to spillover via its Herat and Nimroz supply corridors. ISIS-K struck a mosque in Mazar-e-Sharif and conducted a second attack in Kunduz on the same day. The Af-Pak non-escalation framework marked 12 consecutive days without major cross-border incidents — the longest lull in the conflict — while Urumqi Round 2 is now confirmed for May.
footerNote: Data sourced from UNAMA, SIGAR, ACLED, Brown University Costs of War Project, US DoD, Reuters, BBC, Tolo News, Dawn, Al Jazeera, HRW, OHCHR
